does: This paper introduces XL-SafetyBench, a multilingual safety evaluation suite containing 5,500 test cases spanning 10 country-language pairs designed to assess both adversarial robustness and cultural sensitivity in large language models. The benchmark comprises two components: a Jailbreak Benchmark of country-grounded adversarial prompts and a Cultural Benchmark embedding local sensitivities within seemingly innocuous requests. The authors develop a rigorous multi-stage construction pipeline combining LLM-assisted prompt discovery, automated validation, and dual independent native-speaker annotation per country. They introduce two new metrics—Neutral-Safe Rate (NSR) and Cultural Sensitivity Rate (CSR)—alongside traditional Attack Success Rate (ASR) to distinguish principled refusals from comprehension failures. Evaluating 10 frontier models and 27 local LLMs, they find that jailbreak robustness and cultural awareness operate independently among frontier models, indicating these dimensions of safety require distinct evaluation and optimization.
connects: This work extends the growing research on LLM safety evaluation beyond English-centric benchmarks, building on prior work like AdvBench and other adversarial robustness evaluations that focused primarily on universal harms. It addresses limitations in translation-based approaches to safety assessment by recognizing that country-specific and culturally embedded harms cannot be captured through simple localization. The paper connects to parallel efforts in cross-cultural NLP and fairness research examining how models perform across different languages and communities, while responding to recent findings that safety properties do not uniformly transfer across languages. By distinguishing cultural sensitivity from universal safety, it engages with emerging literature on culturally contextual AI harm and the inadequacy of one-size-fits-all safety frameworks.
enables: This benchmark enables more nuanced safety evaluation of multilingual and local LLMs, supporting development of culturally aware models that responsibly handle region-specific sensitivities without over-censoring. The finding that jailbreak robustness and cultural sensitivity are decoupled suggests future work should develop targeted mitigation strategies for each dimension independently. The framework and metrics can guide safety researchers in designing interventions that strengthen cultural awareness without sacrificing model utility. It opens investigation into why frontier models fail at cultural detection despite strong adversarial robustness, potentially informing training approaches that integrate cultural knowledge. The work establishes infrastructure for continuous evaluation of emerging local models across diverse regions and could motivate development of safety techniques grounded in local cultural and linguistic expertise rather than English-derived universal principles.
